introRows.html: The terrain where a battle takes action is as crucial as the armies involved. Walls block Line of Sight, height offers a commanding fire position, and narrow passages can funnel an entire assault into a killing zone. This part explains how Line of Sight functions, how terrain impacts models of various sizes, and how cover and elevation influence the flow of combat.
introRows.plain: The terrain where a battle takes action is as crucial as the armies involved. Walls block Line of Sight , height offers a commanding fire position, and narrow passages can funnel an entire assault into a killing zone. This part explains how Line of Sight functions, how terrain impacts models of various sizes, and how cover and elevation influence the flow of combat.

rows.html: Every shot, every ability, and every order that requires a clear view of the target follows the same process. Line of Sight determines what a model can see and, by extension, what it can shoot at.
rows.plain: Every shot, every ability, and every order that requires a clear view of the target follows the same process. Line of Sight determines what a model can see and, by extension, what it can shoot at.

rows.html: StarCraft: The Miniatures Game uses a 2D Line of Sight (LoS) system. All Line of Sight checks are resolved from a top-down perspective, looking directly down at the battlefield from above.
rows.plain: StarCraft: The Miniatures Game uses a 2D Line of Sight (LoS) system. All Line of Sight checks are resolved from a top-down perspective, looking directly down at the battlefield from above.

rows.html: To determine whether an acting model can see a target, trace an imaginary straight line from any part of the acting model’s base to any part of the target model’s base.
rows.plain: To determine whether an acting model can see a target, trace an imaginary straight line from any part of the acting model’s base to any part of the target model’s base.

rows.html: If the line does not pass through any Blocking Terrain (any terrain piece with an Effective Size of 1 or greater - see Part 11), the target is Visible. No further checks are required.
rows.plain: If the line does not pass through any Blocking Terrain (any terrain piece with an Effective Size of 1 or greater - see Part 11), the target is Visible . No further checks are required.

rows.html: If the line passes through Blocking Terrain, the target is not automatically hidden. Check the Cover rules (Part 7. 1. 1) to determine whether the terrain actually blocks Line of Sight. Terrain that intersects the trace but does not meet the requirements for Full Cover or Direct Cover does not block Line of Sight. The target remains Visible.
rows.plain: If the line passes through Blocking Terrain , the target is not automatically hidden . Check the Cover rules (Part 7. 1. 1 ) to determine whether the terrain actually blocks Line of Sight . Terrain that intersects the trace but does not meet the requirements for Full Cover or Direct Cover does not block Line of Sight . The target remains Visible .

rows.html: For Line of Sight purposes, a terrain piece’s footprint is defined by its physical base or, if it has no base, the outermost edges of its solid structure viewed from above. Unless players agree otherwise during Battlefield Setup, gaps, windows, doorways, and open interiors within the footprint do block the Line of Sight.
rows.plain: For Line of Sight purposes, a terrain piece’s footprint is defined by its physical base or, if it has no base, the outermost edges of its solid structure viewed from above. Unless players agree otherwise during Battlefield Setup , gaps, windows, doorways, and open interiors within the footprint do block the Line of Sight .

rows.html: A terrain opening may be passable for movement without counting as open for Line of Sight (see Part 4. 6 for Gap Clearance rules).
rows.plain: A terrain opening may be passable for movement without counting as open for Line of Sight (see Part 4. 6 for Gap Clearance rules).

rows.html: Players must agree during Battlefield Setup on the footprint of each terrain piece and on which openings, if any, count as passable gaps for movement.
rows.plain: Players must agree during Battlefield Setup on the footprint of each terrain piece and on which openings, if any, count as passable gaps for movement.

rows.html: Designer’s note: Our game uses a top-down Line of Sight method rather than a precise measurement from the model’s eye. This prevents disputes over exact posture, slouching torsos, and weapon placement. It also ensures terrain interacts correctly regardless of model posture or base design. The downside is a slight loss of cinematic feel, but it is well worth it for speed and balance.
rows.plain: Designer’s note: Our game uses a top-down Line of Sight method rather than a precise measurement from the model’s eye. This prevents disputes over exact posture, slouching torsos, and weapon placement. It also ensures terrain interacts correctly regardless of model posture or base design. The downside is a slight loss of cinematic feel, but it is well worth it for speed and balance.

rows.html: Terrain pieces may block the Line of Sight between models. A terrain piece provides Cover in one of two ways:
rows.plain: Terrain pieces may block the Line of Sight between models . A terrain piece provides Cover in one of two ways:

rows.html: Full Cover: A terrain piece blocks Line of Sight if its Effective Size (Part 7. 1. 2) is equal to or greater than the Effective Size of both the Attacker and the Target. Neither model can see over the terrain - Line of Sight is blocked.
rows.plain: Full Cover : A terrain piece blocks Line of Sight if its Effective Size (Part 7. 1. 2 ) is equal to or greater than the Effective Size of both the Attacker and the Target. Neither model can see over the terrain - Line of Sight is blocked.

rows.html: Direct Cover: If the Line of Sigh t trace passes through a terrain piece, and either the Attacker or the target is Within 1" of that terrain, the Line of Sight between them is blocked, provided the terrain piece' s Effective Size is equal to or greater than the Effective Size of the model that is Within 1".
rows.plain: Direct Cover : If the Line of Sigh t trace passes through a terrain piece, and either the Attacker or the target is Within 1" of that terrain, the Line of Sight between them is blocked, provided the terrain piece' s Effective Size is equal to or greater than the Effective Size of the model that is Within 1" .

rows.html: Exception: If both the Attacker and the target are Within1" of the same terrain piece and Within3" of each other, this rule does not apply. Resolve Line of Sight normally using the standard trace (Part 7. 1).
rows.plain: Exception: If both the Attacker and the target are Within 1" of the same terrain piece and Within 3" of each other, this rule does not apply. Resolve Line of Sight normally using the standard trace (Part 7. 1 ) .

rows.html: Each terrain piece is assessed for Cover independently. Terrain pieces do not combine their Effective Size or proximity. If no single terrain piece meets the requirements for Full Cover or Direct Cover, Line of Sight is not blocked, regardless of how many terrain pieces the trace passes through.
rows.plain: Each terrain piece is assessed for Cover independently. Terrain pieces do not combine their Effective Size or proximity. If no single terrain piece meets the requirements for Full Cover or Direct Cover , Line of Sight is not blocked, regardless of how many terrain pieces the trace passes through.

rows.html: In this example, a player checks Line of Sight from a Goliath (Size 3) to a Unit of five Marines (Size 2) through a ruined wall (Size 2). The wall is too short to block the Goliath’s view entirely - Full Cover fails. However, two Marines are Within1" of the wall. Because the wall’s Size equals the Marines’ Size, the Line of Sight to and from those two Marines is blocked - they are not Visible to the Goliath, and the Goliath is not Visible to them. The remaining three Marines are too far from the wall and are Visible normally.
rows.plain: In this example, a player checks Line of Sight from a Goliath (Size 3) to a Unit of five Marines (Size 2) through a ruined wall (Size 2). The wall is too short to block the Goliath ’s view entirely - Full Cover fails. However, two Marines are Within 1" of the wall. Because the wall’s Size equals the Marines’ Size, the Line of Sight to and from those two Marines is blocked - they are not Visible to the Goliath , and the Goliath is not Visible to them. The remaining three Marines are too far from the wall and are Visible normally.

rows.html: Elevation Dead Zone If a model on HIGH GROUND (Size 3+) draws Line of Sight to a model at GROUND LEVEL that is Within 1" of the base of the same terrain piece, Line of Sight is blocked. This applies in both directions - neither model can see the other.
rows.plain: Elevation Dead Zone If a model on HIGH GROUND (Size 3+) draws Line of Sight to a model at GROUND LEVEL that is Within 1" of the base of the same terrain piece, Line of Sight is blocked. This applies in both directions - neither model can see the other.

rows.html: Exception: If both models are Within 1" of the same terrain piece and Within 3" of each other, this rule does not apply. Resolve Line of Sight normally using the standard trace (Part 7. 1).
rows.plain: Exception: If both models are Within 1" of the same terrain piece and Within 3" of each other, this rule does not apply. Resolve Line of Sight normally using the standard trace (Part 7. 1 ).

rows.html: In this example: a Marine onHIGH GROUND checksLine of Sight to a Zergling atGROUND LEVEL. The Zergling isWithin 1"of the base of the same terrain piece the Marine is standing on, but the two models are more than 3" apart. The Zergling is too close to the foot of the cliff to be seen from above -Line of Sight is blocked. This works in both directions - the Zergling cannot see the Marine above it either. If the two models wereWithin 3"of each other, Close Quarters would apply, and standardLine of Sight rules would be used instead.
rows.plain: In this example: a Marine on HIGH GROUND checks Line of Sight to a Zergling at GROUND LEVEL . The Zergling is Within 1" of the base of the same terrain piece the Marine is standing on, but the two models are more than 3" apart. The Zergling is too close to the foot of the cliff to be seen from above - Line of Sight is blocked. This works in both directions - the Zergling cannot see the Marine above it either. If the two models were Within 3" of each other, Close Quarters would apply, and standard Line of Sight rules would be used instead.

rows.html: When a model stands on top of a terrain piece (such as HIGH GROUND):
rows.plain: When a model stands on top of a terrain piece (such as HIGH GROUND ):

rows.html: Effective Size is equal to the model Size plus the terrain Size.
rows.plain: Effective Size is equal to the model Size plus the terrain Size.

rows.html: EXAMPLE: A Marine (Size 2) on HIGH GROUND (Size 4) has an Effective Size of 6. He can see over Size 5 walls that would blind him at GROUND LEVEL.
rows.plain: EXAMPLE: A Marine (Size 2) on HIGH GROUND (Size 4) has an Effective Size of 6. He can see over Size 5 walls that would blind him at GROUND LEVEL .

rows.html: Abathur’s TIP: HIGH GROUND is a powerhouse on the battlefield. When a Unit is on terrain with a Size of 3 or 4, the Effective Size of that Unit goes through the roof, letting it peer over barriers that would block the Line of Sight of Units on lower ground. The best position for ranged support is to utilise HIGH GROUND, as you can rain down fire from above. The downside, of course, is that Units on HIGH GROUND are exposed to just about everything, so only put Units there if they can handle whatever is coming their way. Ranged specialists with good Armour or Evade stats are the ones that really shine from HIGH GROUND, but Units with lower stats should remain on the ground if they want to stay hidden.
rows.plain: Abathur’s TIP: HIGH GROUND is a powerhouse on the battlefield. When a Unit is on terrain with a Size of 3 or 4, the Effective Size of that Unit goes through the roof, letting it peer over barriers that would block the Line of Sight of Units on lower ground. The best position for ranged support is to utilise HIGH GROUND, as you can rain down fire from above. The downside, of course, is that Units on HIGH GROUND are exposed to just about everything, so only put Units there if they can handle whatever is coming their way. Ranged specialists with good Armour or Evade stats are the ones that really shine from HIGH GROUND , but Units with lower stats should remain on the ground if they want to stay hidden .

rows.html: Top-Down Perspective: Only terrain pieces that rise vertically between the two models' positions (as viewed from above) can block the shot. The horizontal surface a model stands on does not obstruct the top-down trace.
rows.plain: Top-Down Perspective: Only terrain pieces that rise vertically between the two models ' positions (as viewed from above) can block the shot. The horizontal surface a model stands on does not obstruct the top-down trace.

rows.html: Stacking Terrain: When a terrain piece is set on HIGH GROUND or MID GROUND, its Effective Size increases in the same way as a model’s - it gains the Size of the terrain it stands on.
rows.plain: Stacking Terrain: When a terrain piece is set on HIGH GROUND or MID GROUND , its Effective Size increases in the same way as a model’s - it gains the Size of the terrain it stands on.

rows.html: In this example,a Marauder (Size 2) is onHIGH GROUND (Size 3), giving it an Effective Size of 5. A ruined wall (Size 2) is also onHIGH GROUND (Size 3), giving it an Effective Size of 5. Because they have the same Effective Size, the Marauder cannot see over the ruined wall. The Roach (Size 2) atGROUND LEVEL cannot make aLine of Sight with the Marauder because the ruined wall (Effective Size 5) blocks it. However, the Hydralisk atGROUND LEVEL can make a Line of Sight with the Marauder because there is no obstacle in the way.
rows.plain: In this example, a Marauder (Size 2) is on HIGH GROUND (Size 3), giving it an Effective Size of 5. A ruined wall (Size 2) is also on HIGH GROUND (Size 3), giving it an Effective Size of 5. Because they have the same Effective Size , the Marauder cannot see over the ruined wall. The Roach (Size 2) at GROUND LEVEL cannot make a Line of Sight with the Marauder because the ruined wall ( Effective Size 5) blocks it. However, the Hydralisk at GROUND LEVEL can make a Line of Sight with the Marauder because there is no obstacle in the way.

rows.html: In this example, the Goliath (Size 3) stands onHIGH GROUND (Size 4), increasing the Goliath’s Effective Size to 7. The wall (Size 2) set onHIGH GROUND has an Effective Size of 6.
rows.plain: In this example, the Goliath (Size 3) stands on HIGH GROUND (Size 4), increasing the Goliath ’s Effective Size to 7. The wall (Size 2) set on HIGH GROUND has an Effective Size of 6.

rows.html: If all models of a Unit are standing on HIGH GROUND (Size 3 or larger terrain) with their entire bases, the Unit becomes eligible to make an Evade Roll against all Ranged Attacks originating from a lower elevation. The attack originates from the lower elevation if at least one model of the attacking Unit isn’t standing on the HIGH GROUND with their entire base.
rows.plain: If all models of a Unit are standing on HIGH GROUND (Size 3 or larger terrain) with their entire bases , the Unit becomes eligible to make an Evade Roll against all Ranged Attacks originating from a lower elevation. The attack originates from the lower elevation if at least one model of the attacking Unit isn’t standing on the HIGH GROUND with their entire base.

rows.html: Exception: Flying Units never benefit from HIGH GROUND Cover, regardless of their physical position on the battlefield. Ranged Attacks made by Flying Units never originate from the lower elevation, regardless of their position on the battlefield.
rows.plain: Exception: Flying Units never benefit from HIGH GROUND Cover , regardless of their physical position on the battlefield. Ranged Attacks made by Flying Units never originate from the lower elevation, regardless of their position on the battlefield.

rows.html: DESIGNER’S NOTE: HIGH GROUND is already a strong position on the battlefield, thanks to the bonus from Effective Size and Line of Sight. However, a Unit occupying HIGH GROUND is also more Visible to almost everything on the table, creating a weakness. The Evade Roll provided to Units occupying HIGH GROUND reflects the defensive benefit of being in a raised position. It is more difficult to fire uphill. This provides players with a strong reason to occupy a raised position, aside from the Line of Sight bonus.
rows.plain: DESIGNER’S NOTE: HIGH GROUND is already a strong position on the battlefield, thanks to the bonus from Effective Size and Line of Sight . However, a Unit occupying HIGH GROUND is also more Visible to almost everything on the table, creating a weakness. The Evade Roll provided to Units occupying HIGH GROUND reflects the defensive benefit of being in a raised position. It is more difficult to fire uphill. This provides players with a strong reason to occupy a raised position, aside from the Line of Sight bonus.

rows.html: When checking Line of Sigh t to or from a Flying model, ignore the Full Cover rule.
rows.plain: When checking Line of Sigh t to or from a Flying model, ignore the Full Cover rule.

rows.html: Direct Cover and the Elevation Dead Zone rules still apply.
rows.plain: Direct Cover and the Elevation Dead Zone rules still apply.

rows.html: For Cover purposes, treat a Flying model’s Effective Size as higher than the Effective Size of any terrain piece on the table.
rows.plain: For Cover purposes, treat a Flying model’s Effective Size as higher than the Effective Size of any terrain piece on the table.

rows.html: Because of this, terrain can never provide Full Cover against a Flying model and can never provide Direct Cover to a Flying model as the model that is Within 1" of terrain. However, a Flying model still obeys any Direct Cover or Elevation Dead Zone rule that applies to the other model in the attack.
rows.plain: Because of this, terrain can never provide Full Cover against a Flying model and can never provide Direct Cover to a Flying model as the model that is Within 1" of terrain. However, a Flying model still obeys any Direct Cover or Elevation Dead Zone rule that applies to the other model in the attack.

rows.html: The Engagement Range extends 1" horizontally from any model’s base. When two Enemy models are Within 1" of each other, they are Engaged - locked in close combat.
rows.plain: The Engagement Range extends 1" horizontally from any model’s base. When two Enemy models are Within 1" of each other, they are Engaged - locked in close combat.

rows.html: When any model in a Unit is Within 1" of any model in an Enemy Unit, both Units are considered Engaged.
rows.plain: When any model in a Unit is Within 1" of any model in an Enemy Unit, both Units are considered Engaged .

rows.html: Models are Engaged only when all of the following conditions are met:
rows.plain: Models are Engaged only when all of the following conditions are met:

rows.html: Combat Tags match:Ground models Engage only Ground models. Flying modelscannot be Engaged by any model.
rows.plain: Combat Tags match: Ground models Engage only Ground models . Flying models cannot be Engaged by any model.

rows.html: Terrain does not block:
rows.plain: Terrain does not block:

rows.html: Size 0–1 terrain: Engagement is possible across it.
rows.plain: Size 0–1 terrain: Engagement is possible across it.

rows.html: Size 2+ terrain: Modelscannot Engage through it. Even Within 1", a Size 2+ wall between them means they are not Engaged.
rows.plain: Size 2+ terrain: Models cannot Engage through it. Even Within 1" , a Size 2+ wall between them means they are not Engaged .

rows.html: Models on HIGH GROUNDcannot Engage models on GROUND LEVEL, and vice versa.
rows.plain: Models on HIGH GROUND cannot Engage models on GROUND LEVEL , and vice versa.

rows.html: Models on MID GROUND can Engage models on HIGH GROUND or GROUND LEVEL only if both models are adjacent to the same ACCESS POINT connecting their respective elevations
rows.plain: Models on MID GROUND can Engage models on HIGH GROUND or GROUND LEVEL only if both models are adjacent to the same ACCESS POINT connecting their respective elevations

rows.html: Tokens represent tangible battlefield assets - Creep Tumors, Forcefields, and Auto-Turrets. Treat them as Terrain (Size 0) with their own base. Models may move through Tokens, but cannot end movement overlapping them unless specified. Tokens are removed at the end of the Game Round unless stated otherwise. Always measure to the closest base edge of a Token.
rows.plain: Tokens represent tangible battlefield assets - Creep Tumors, Forcefields, and Auto-Turrets. Treat them as Terrain (Size 0) with their own base. Models may move through Tokens , but cannot end movement overlapping them unless specified. Tokens are removed at the end of the Game Round unless stated otherwise. Always measure to the closest base edge of a Token.

rows.html: Markers are used to keep track of status or game states. Unlike Tokens, they have no physical presence. They do not block Line of Sight or Movement.
rows.plain: Markers are used to keep track of status or game states. Unlike Tokens , they have no physical presence. They do not block Line of Sight or Movement.

rows.html: Mission Markers are numbered Markers used on the battlefield during Setup at coordinates shown on the Deployment Card. Each Marker is 32mm in diameter and has two sides: Activated or Deactivated. Each one is colour-coded by number: Markers 1 & 3 are Red, Markers 2 & 4 are Blue, and Marker 5 is Neutral.
rows.plain: Mission Markers are numbered Markers used on the battlefield during Setup at coordinates shown on the Deployment Card . Each Marker is 32mm in diameter and has two sides: Activated or Deactivated . Each one is colour-coded by number: Markers 1 & 3 are Red, Markers 2 & 4 are Blue, and Marker 5 is Neutral.

rows.html: Activation Markers are set beside the Unit that has completed its turn in a Phase. They are double-sided with the arrow facing upwards, showing that the Unit has finished its turn in Phase 1 (Movement). The reverse side indicates that the Unit has completed its turn in Phase 2 (Assault).
rows.plain: Activation Markers are set beside the Unit that has completed its turn in a Phase. They are double-sided with the arrow facing upwards, showing that the Unit has finished its turn in Phase 1 (Movement). The reverse side indicates that the Unit has completed its turn in Phase 2 (Assault).

rows.html: Faction Indicators are used on Mission Markers to show which player controls that Mission Marker at any moment. Some Special Abilities utilise Faction Indicators to mark an area of the battlefield.
rows.plain: Faction Indicators are used on Mission Markers to show which player controls that Mission Marker at any moment. Some Special Abilities utilise Faction Indicators to mark an area of the battlefield.

rows.html: Zone of Influence Markers are used to define the Zone of Influence when the Entry Edge doesn’t extend along the entire table edge. These are L-shaped corner markers that must be set exactly as shown on the Deployment Card.
rows.plain: Zone of Influence Markers are used to define the Zone of Influence when the Entry Edge doesn’t extend along the entire table edge. These are L-shaped corner markers that must be set exactly as shown on the Deployment Card .

rows.html: First Player Marker is used to track which player has initiative. The holder chooses which player activates first at the beginning of each Phase. The Marker passes between players throughout the game, usually when a player Passes early in a Phase or when Victory Points are tallied at the end of the Round.
rows.plain: First Player Marker is used to track which player has initiative. The holder chooses which player activates first at the beginning of each Phase. The Marker passes between players throughout the game, usually when a player Passes early in a Phase or when Victory Points are tallied at the end of the Round.

rows.html: When the last model falls, the Unit is Destroyed.
rows.plain: When the last model falls, the Unit is Destroyed.

rows.html: Every effect, ability, and condition currently on the Destroyed Unit immediately ends. Tokens created by the destroyed Unit are removed (unless they are STAY IN PLAY). Effects that the Destroyed Unit applied to other Units remain on those Units unless the effect explicitly states otherwise.
rows.plain: Every effect, ability, and condition currently on the Destroyed Unit immediately ends. Tokens created by the destroyed Unit are removed (unless they are STAY IN PLAY ). Effects that the Destroyed Unit applied to other Units remain on those Units unless the effect explicitly states otherwise.

rows.html: A Destroyed Unit cannot return to play unless a specific rule states otherwise.
rows.plain: A Destroyed Unit cannot return to play unless a specific rule states otherwise.
